meta.notes: General-chemistry MCQ scraped verbatim from the chapter 'Multiple-Choice Questions' sections. The GOB book's Chapter 16 answer key lists answers for ALL ten MCQ in each chapter (not just odds), so every item here is keyed to source-answer-key and was additionally cross-checked against the source text. Mirrors the cpbs-001..004 items already in questions.json (same book, later chapters).

questions.stem: Which of the following has a positive charge?
questions.explanation: A proton carries a +1 elementary charge. Electrons are negatively charged, neutrons are neutral, and a neutral hydrogen atom has no net charge.
questions.sourceName: LibreTexts — General, Organic, and Biochemistry with Problems, Case Studies, and Activities (Roosevelt University), Ch. 2 Atoms and Elements
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. correctAnswer from the course's Chapter 16 answer key (16.2 MCQ: Q1 = a).

questions.stem: Which of the following is the mass number for the isotope Br-79 (79Br)?
questions.explanation: In the notation 79Br the leading number is the mass number (protons + neutrons) = 79. 35 is bromine's atomic number, 44 is the neutron count (79 - 35), and 79.904 is the element's average atomic mass.
questions.sourceName: LibreTexts — General, Organic, and Biochemistry with Problems, Case Studies, and Activities (Roosevelt University), Ch. 2 Atoms and Elements
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. correctAnswer from the course's Chapter 16 answer key (16.2 MCQ: Q2 = c).

questions.stem: How many electrons are present in Mg2+?
questions.explanation: A neutral magnesium atom has 12 electrons; the 2+ cation has lost 2 electrons, leaving 10.
questions.sourceName: LibreTexts — General, Organic, and Biochemistry with Problems, Case Studies, and Activities (Roosevelt University), Ch. 2 Atoms and Elements
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. correctAnswer from the course's Chapter 16 answer key (16.2 MCQ: Q3 = a).

questions.stem: Which of the following is the correct set of protons, neutrons, and electrons for Cd-113 as the 2+ cation (113Cd2+)?
questions.explanation: Cadmium has atomic number 48 (48 protons). Mass number 113 means 113 - 48 = 65 neutrons. A 2+ charge means two fewer electrons than protons: 46 electrons.
questions.sourceName: LibreTexts — General, Organic, and Biochemistry with Problems, Case Studies, and Activities (Roosevelt University), Ch. 2 Atoms and Elements
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. correctAnswer from the course's Chapter 16 answer key (16.2 MCQ: Q4 = c).

questions.stem: Which of the following is the correct chemical symbol for copper?
questions.explanation: Copper's symbol is Cu (from the Latin cuprum). C is carbon, Co is cobalt, and Cr is chromium.
questions.sourceName: LibreTexts — General, Organic, and Biochemistry with Problems, Case Studies, and Activities (Roosevelt University), Ch. 2 Atoms and Elements
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. correctAnswer from the course's Chapter 16 answer key (16.2 MCQ: Q5 = d).

questions.stem: Select the correct atomic symbol for an isotope of nitrogen with 8 neutrons.
questions.explanation: Nitrogen has 7 protons; with 8 neutrons the mass number is 7 + 8 = 15, written 15N (N-15). The leading superscript is the mass number, not the proton or neutron count alone.
questions.sourceName: LibreTexts — General, Organic, and Biochemistry with Problems, Case Studies, and Activities (Roosevelt University), Ch. 2 Atoms and Elements
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. correctAnswer from the course's Chapter 16 answer key (16.2 MCQ: Q6 = d).

questions.stem: The average atomic mass of chlorine is 35.4527 amu. There are two stable isotopes of chlorine: Cl-35 and Cl-37. Given this information, which of the statements below is true?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The fractional abundance of Cl-35 is greater than that of Cl-37.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The fractional abundance of Cl-37 is greater than that of Cl-35.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The fractional abundances of Cl-35 and Cl-37 are equal.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The relative fractional abundances of Cl-35 and Cl-37 cannot be determined from this information.

questions.explanation: A weighted-average atomic mass lies closest to the mass of the most abundant isotope. Because 35.45 is much nearer 35 than 37, Cl-35 must be the more abundant isotope.
questions.sourceName: LibreTexts — General, Organic, and Biochemistry with Problems, Case Studies, and Activities (Roosevelt University), Ch. 2 Atoms and Elements
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. correctAnswer from the course's Chapter 16 answer key (16.2 MCQ: Q7 = a).

questions.stem: How many valence electrons does an atom of phosphorus have?
questions.explanation: Phosphorus is in group 15 (VA) of the periodic table, so a neutral P atom has 5 valence electrons. (15 is its total electron count, not its valence count.)
questions.sourceName: LibreTexts — General, Organic, and Biochemistry with Problems, Case Studies, and Activities (Roosevelt University), Ch. 2 Atoms and Elements
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. correctAnswer from the course's Chapter 16 answer key (16.2 MCQ: Q8 = b).

questions.stem: To achieve an octet in its valence shell, sulfur must …
questions.explanation: Sulfur is in group 16 with 6 valence electrons, so it must gain 2 electrons to reach a stable octet of 8, forming the sulfide ion S2-.
questions.sourceName: LibreTexts — General, Organic, and Biochemistry with Problems, Case Studies, and Activities (Roosevelt University), Ch. 2 Atoms and Elements
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. correctAnswer from the course's Chapter 16 answer key (16.2 MCQ: Q9 = c).

questions.stem: Based on your knowledge of the periodic variation in atomic size, which of the following correctly orders the elements in terms of decreasing atomic radius?
questions.explanation: Atomic radius decreases from left to right across a period (increasing effective nuclear charge). For the period-2 elements B, N, and F, decreasing radius is therefore B > N > F.
questions.sourceName: LibreTexts — General, Organic, and Biochemistry with Problems, Case Studies, and Activities (Roosevelt University), Ch. 2 Atoms and Elements
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. correctAnswer from the course's Chapter 16 answer key (16.2 MCQ: Q10 = a).

questions.stem: Which atom is likely to form a cation?
questions.explanation: Potassium is an alkali metal (group 1); metals readily lose electrons to form cations (K+). Oxygen and bromine are nonmetals that tend to form anions, and argon is a noble gas that is generally unreactive.
questions.sourceName: LibreTexts — General, Organic, and Biochemistry with Problems, Case Studies, and Activities (Roosevelt University), Ch. 3 Ionic and Covalent Compounds
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. correctAnswer from the course's Chapter 16 answer key (16.3 MCQ: Q1 = d).

questions.stem: Which is not a physical property of metals?
questions.explanation: Metals are good conductors (not insulators) of electricity, and are also good heat conductors, malleable, and ductile. 'Good insulator of electricity' is therefore not a property of metals.
questions.sourceName: LibreTexts — General, Organic, and Biochemistry with Problems, Case Studies, and Activities (Roosevelt University), Ch. 3 Ionic and Covalent Compounds
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. correctAnswer from the course's Chapter 16 answer key (16.3 MCQ: Q2 = b).

questions.stem: What is the charge on sulfate?
questions.explanation: Sulfate is the polyatomic ion SO4^2-, which carries a 2- charge.
questions.sourceName: LibreTexts — General, Organic, and Biochemistry with Problems, Case Studies, and Activities (Roosevelt University), Ch. 3 Ionic and Covalent Compounds
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. correctAnswer from the course's Chapter 16 answer key (16.3 MCQ: Q3 = d).

questions.stem: Which element is a nonmetal?
questions.explanation: Sulfur (S) is a nonmetal. Lithium and calcium are metals (groups 1 and 2), and zinc is a transition metal.
questions.sourceName: LibreTexts — General, Organic, and Biochemistry with Problems, Case Studies, and Activities (Roosevelt University), Ch. 3 Ionic and Covalent Compounds
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. correctAnswer from the course's Chapter 16 answer key (16.3 MCQ: Q4 = b).

questions.stem: What is the correct chemical formula for the ionic compound formed between calcium and oxygen?
questions.explanation: Calcium forms Ca2+ and oxygen forms O2-. The 2+ and 2- charges balance in a 1:1 ratio, giving the empirical formula CaO.
questions.sourceName: LibreTexts — General, Organic, and Biochemistry with Problems, Case Studies, and Activities (Roosevelt University), Ch. 3 Ionic and Covalent Compounds
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. correctAnswer from the course's Chapter 16 answer key (16.3 MCQ: Q5 = a).

questions.stem: What is the correct name of AlP?
questions.explanation: Aluminum has a single fixed charge of 3+, so no Roman numeral is needed. The monatomic anion of phosphorus is phosphide (P3-), so AlP is aluminum phosphide. Phosphite and phosphate are oxoanions, not present here.
questions.sourceName: LibreTexts — General, Organic, and Biochemistry with Problems, Case Studies, and Activities (Roosevelt University), Ch. 3 Ionic and Covalent Compounds
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. correctAnswer from the course's Chapter 16 answer key (16.3 MCQ: Q6 = a).

questions.stem: What is the correct name of P2O5?
questions.explanation: P2O5 is a binary molecular (nonmetal-nonmetal) compound named with Greek prefixes: di- for the 2 phosphorus atoms and penta- for the 5 oxygen atoms, giving diphosphorus pentoxide.
questions.sourceName: LibreTexts — General, Organic, and Biochemistry with Problems, Case Studies, and Activities (Roosevelt University), Ch. 3 Ionic and Covalent Compounds
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. correctAnswer from the course's Chapter 16 answer key (16.3 MCQ: Q7 = b).

questions.stem: What is the correct formula for nitrous acid?
questions.explanation: The '-ous' acid corresponds to the '-ite' oxoanion. Nitrite is NO2-, so nitrous acid is HNO2. HNO3 (from nitrate, NO3-) is nitric acid.
questions.sourceName: LibreTexts — General, Organic, and Biochemistry with Problems, Case Studies, and Activities (Roosevelt University), Ch. 3 Ionic and Covalent Compounds
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. correctAnswer from the course's Chapter 16 answer key (16.3 MCQ: Q8 = c).

questions.stem: What is the correct name of Cr2S3?
questions.explanation: Sulfide is S2-, so three sulfides total -6. The two chromium ions must therefore total +6, i.e., Cr3+. Because chromium is a variable-charge metal, the name uses a Roman numeral: chromium(III) sulfide.
questions.sourceName: LibreTexts — General, Organic, and Biochemistry with Problems, Case Studies, and Activities (Roosevelt University), Ch. 3 Ionic and Covalent Compounds
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. correctAnswer from the course's Chapter 16 answer key (16.3 MCQ: Q9 = d).

questions.stem: What is the correct formula for ammonium sulfate?
questions.explanation: Ammonium is NH4+ and sulfate is SO4^2-. Two ammonium ions are needed to balance one sulfate, and the polyatomic cation is enclosed in parentheses: (NH4)2SO4.
questions.sourceName: LibreTexts — General, Organic, and Biochemistry with Problems, Case Studies, and Activities (Roosevelt University), Ch. 3 Ionic and Covalent Compounds
